title: Efficient Many-Body Shadow Metrology via Clifford Lensing
summary_bullets: Using experimentally accessible Clifford operations acting jointly on states and observables, the authors introduce "Clifford lensing"—θ-independent Clifford isometries that coherently refocus distributed many-body phase information onto a reduced subsystem while preserving the quantum Fisher information and expectation values. They prove constructive results linking [n,1] stabilizer codes to deterministic Clifford phase kickback, characterize when full localization to one qubit (stabilizer-compatible) versus partial localization to k qubits (stabilizer-incompatible) is possible, and show that Clifford lensing reduces the Pauli weight of the symmetric logarithmic derivative (SLD) to enable efficient classical-shadow estimation. The framework is implemented experimentally with liquid-state NMR up to 15 qubits, introducing metrologically sufficient channels and partial shadow tomography under restricted collective control and demonstrating near–Heisenberg-limited phase sensitivity with greatly reduced measurement complexity.

Why Yanbei would care: this work directly addresses the gap between formal optimal measurements (SLD eigenbases) and experimentally accessible measurements in many-body interferometry—a central theme in his work on quantum measurement theory, quantum noise in interferometers, back-action evasion, and optomechanics. The explicit mapping between error-correcting codes and interferometric phase kickback (Clifford lensing) provides a toolbox for concentrating weak, distributed phase signals onto few degrees of freedom, which is valuable for designing feasible Heisenberg-scaling probes and readouts in constrained platforms (including proposals for tabletop probes of gravity-induced phases or decoherence). Moreover, the metrologically sufficient channels and shadow protocols show how quantum-information methods (Clifford circuits, shadows, stabilizer techniques) can be used to reduce measurement complexity in realistic experiments—aligning with his interests in combining QIS techniques with precision sensing and foundational tests.

Keywords/topics: Clifford lensing, symmetric logarithmic derivative (SLD), quantum Fisher information, stabilizer codes / phase kickback, Pauli-weight reduction, classical shadows / partial shadow tomography, metrologically sufficient channels, collective Clifford control, NMR demonstration (≤15 qubits). Published: arXiv v1 25 Mar 2026 (very recent).
